description: ClawHQ backlog — derived from PRODUCT.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, README.md (2026-03-18)

items.title: clawhq deploy update — push config and version updates to cloud-deployed agents
items.intent: A sovereign cloud agent that cannot be updated is a liability, not an asset. clawhq deploy update must let users push config changes, version upgrades, and new skills to any cloud-deployed agent without reprovisioning. The agent grows pillar must hold for cloud deployments.
items.description: After clawhq deploy provisions a cloud agent, users need to push updates without reprovisioning from scratch. Three update scenarios: (1) config change — update blueprint vars or credentials without restart, (2) clawhq version upgrade — pull latest, rebuild Docker image, restart agent, (3) skill install/remove — evolve the cloud agent remotely. Implementation: SSH keypair injected at provision time (cloud-init). clawhq deploy update --id <agent-id> connects via SSH, pulls new config or runs clawhq update/skill commands on the remote. Requires deploy state file to track SSH endpoint and keypair location. Works in zero-trust mode — user initiates, no inbound from cloud service.
acceptanceCriteria: clawhq deploy update --id <agent-id> --config pushes updated blueprint vars to the remote agent without reprovisioning
clawhq deploy update --id <agent-id> --version pulls latest clawhq, rebuilds Docker image, and restarts the agent on the remote
clawhq deploy update --id <agent-id> --skill install <source> installs a skill on the remote agent
SSH keypair is read from the deploy state file — no manual key management by user
Update fails cleanly if agent-id not found in deploy state — helpful error shown
items.groomingNotes: DEPENDENCY MET: CQ-047 shipped 2026-03-24 (sprints.jsonl confirms). Deploy state file format and SSH keypair injection are now defined. FEAT-005 can proceed. Verify before starting: (1) ~/.clawhq/cloud/instances.json format includes SSH keypair path + instance IP + provider as specified in CQ-047 groomingNotes. (2) SSH keypair was injected at provision time via cloud-init. THREE SUBCOMMANDS remain as specified: (a) deploy update config — rsync vars, restart; (b) deploy update version — SSH in, run clawhq update, verify health; (c) deploy update skill — SSH in, run clawhq skill install. All user-initiated (zero-trust). Priority SHOULD is correct.

items.title: Add skill system section to docs/ARCHITECTURE.md
items.intent: A contributor or evaluator reading ARCHITECTURE.md must understand the full system — the skill system is a core architectural layer that is currently undocumented there.
items.description: docs/ARCHITECTURE.md is nearly production-ready and covers platform, blueprints, security, deployment, modules, and architectural decisions. But the skill system — a core runtime layer — has no dedicated section. Skills are documented individually in configs/skills/*/SKILL.md and configs/skills/*/config.yaml but the architectural pattern (declarative skill definition, cron trigger, prompt templates, boundary enforcement, approval gates) is never explained as a system. Add a 'Layer 3: Skills' section (or 'The Skill System') to ARCHITECTURE.md between the Blueprint and Security sections. Source content from: the 6 existing SKILL.md files (email-digest, investor-update, market-scan, meal-plan, morning-brief, schedule-guard) for the pattern, and the 6 config.yaml files for the schema (schedule, model, dependencies, approval, boundaries). The section should explain: what a skill is, the skill lifecycle (trigger → prompt → execute → boundary check → approval gate), the config.yaml schema, and how skills compose with blueprints.
steps: Read docs/ARCHITECTURE.md to understand the current structure and find the right insertion point (after Layer 2: Blueprints, before Security Architecture)
Read configs/skills/email-digest/SKILL.md and configs/skills/email-digest/config.yaml as the reference skill implementation
Read configs/skills/morning-brief/SKILL.md and configs/skills/morning-brief/config.yaml for a second pattern example
Read at least 2 more configs/skills/*/config.yaml files to confirm the schema is consistent across skills

Add a new section to docs/ARCHITECTURE.md covering: what a skill is (declarative capability definition), skill lifecycle (trigger → prompt template → model execution → boundary enforcement → approval gate if configured), config.yaml schema reference (schedule, model, dependencies, approval, boundaries fields), how blueprints compose skills (blueprint YAML references skill names), skill isolation and boundary enforcement
Ensure the new section follows the same tone and depth as the existing Blueprint and Platform sections
acceptanceCriteria: docs/ARCHITECTURE.md contains a skill system section explaining: what skills are, the skill lifecycle, config.yaml schema, blueprint-skill composition, and boundary enforcement
The section is sourced from existing SKILL.md and config.yaml files — no invented claims
The section is positioned logically within the document (between blueprints and security, or after blueprints)
Existing content in ARCHITECTURE.md is not altered beyond the insertion
items.groomingNotes: Documentation authoring task. Content sources confirmed: 6 SKILL.md files and 6 config.yaml files in configs/skills/, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md (insertion point after blueprints, before security). Steps are implementation-ready. No code changes. No dependencies. Marking readyForSprint=true as requested by PO.

items.title: Create docs/QUICKSTART.md — install to working agent in under 10 minutes
items.intent: A new user must go from nothing to a working personal AI agent using a single document — if they need to read the README, ARCHITECTURE.md, and PRODUCT.md to get started, they won't.
items.description: No quickstart document exists. Content fragments are scattered across: README.md (install commands: curl one-liner and git clone, clawhq init --guided/--smart, clawhq doctor, clawhq status), docs/ARCHITECTURE.md (Install Phases: install → configure → harden → test → launch, Engine Acquisition: trusted cache vs from-source), CLAUDE.md (CLI Commands: full command listing with descriptions), and docs/PRODUCT.md (What Your Day Looks Like: before/after narrative showing the user experience). The quickstart must walk through: prerequisites (Docker, Node.js >=22, Ollama for local models), install (curl or git clone), init wizard (--guided walkthrough with expected output for each step), pick a blueprint (email-manager as the default first experience), connect integrations (IMAP/SMTP for email-manager), launch (clawhq up), verify (clawhq status, clawhq doctor), first interaction (send a message via Signal/Telegram/Discord). Include expected terminal output at each step. The email-manager blueprint is the best first experience because email is universal and the blueprint is fully defined.
steps: Read README.md for install commands (curl, git clone), init commands (--guided, --smart), and post-install commands (doctor, status)
Read docs/ARCHITECTURE.md Install Phases section and Engine Acquisition section for the installation flow
Read docs/PRODUCT.md 'What Your Day Looks Like' section for the user experience narrative
Read CLAUDE.md CLI Commands section for the full command listing
Read configs/blueprints/email-manager.yaml to understand what the first blueprint configures
Read backlog/GAP-ANALYSIS.md for prerequisites and known gaps that affect the quickstart

Compose docs/QUICKSTART.md with sections: Prerequisites (Docker, Node.js >=22, Ollama optional), Install (curl one-liner + from-source option), Initialize (clawhq init --guided walkthrough with expected terminal output), Pick Your First Blueprint (email-manager, what it configures, why start here), Connect Integrations (IMAP/SMTP setup for email-manager), Launch (clawhq up + what to expect), Verify (clawhq status + clawhq doctor), Your First Interaction (send a test message), What's Next (links to ARCHITECTURE.md, CONFIGURATION.md)
Add expected terminal output blocks after every command
Keep under 250 lines — this is a quickstart, not a manual
acceptanceCriteria: docs/QUICKSTART.md exists and covers: prerequisites, install, init, first blueprint, connect integrations, launch, verify, first interaction
Each step includes the exact command and expected terminal output
The email-manager blueprint is used as the first-run example throughout
A user following only this document can go from zero to a working agent
The document is under 250 lines
items.groomingNotes: Documentation authoring task. Content sources confirmed: README.md, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md, docs/PRODUCT.md, CLAUDE.md, configs/blueprints/email-manager.yaml. No dependencies. 250-line cap is appropriate. email-manager is the right first-run blueprint. Marking readyForSprint=true as requested by PO.

items.title: Create docs/CONFIGURATION.md — blueprint schema, skill schema, every config option
items.intent: Users writing custom blueprints or skills must have a single reference document — reading 7 example YAMLs and reverse-engineering the schema is not documentation.
items.description: Configuration content exists but only as examples and scattered references: configs/blueprints/*.yaml (7 blueprints establishing the blueprint schema by example), configs/skills/*/config.yaml (6 skills establishing the skill schema by example), configs/skills/*/SKILL.md (6 behavior definitions establishing the SKILL.md format by example), docs/ARCHITECTURE.md ('What a Blueprint Configures' 10-dimension table, 'The 14 Landmine Rules' table, 'What Gets Generated' directory tree), docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md (openclaw.json reference section 10, Configuration Surface Inventory section 11, The 14 Configuration Landmines section 12 with detailed explanations, Automation section 14 for cron config), CLAUDE.md (Key Design Constraints, CLI Commands). The unified document must cover: blueprint YAML schema (field-by-field, every dimension from the 10-dimension table), skill config.yaml schema (field-by-field: schedule, model, dependencies, approval, boundaries), SKILL.md authoring format, the 14 landmine rules with explanations, model routing configuration, and integration configuration patterns.
steps: Read configs/blueprints/email-manager.yaml and configs/blueprints/research-copilot.yaml as reference blueprints to extract the full field set
Read configs/skills/email-digest/config.yaml and configs/skills/morning-brief/config.yaml as reference skills to extract the full field set
Read configs/skills/email-digest/SKILL.md as reference for the SKILL.md authoring format
Read docs/ARCHITECTURE.md sections: 'What a Blueprint Configures' (10-dimension table), 'The 14 Landmine Rules', 'What Gets Generated'
Read docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md sections 10 (openclaw.json), 11 (Configuration Surface Inventory), 12 (14 Configuration Landmines with detailed explanations), 14 (cron/heartbeat/hooks)
Read CLAUDE.md Key Design Constraints section

Compose docs/CONFIGURATION.md with sections: Blueprint Schema (every field, type, required/optional, description, default), Skill Schema (config.yaml field-by-field + SKILL.md format guide), The 14 Landmine Rules (sourced from OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md section 12, each rule with explanation and safe default), Model Routing (local vs cloud, per-task-type routing), Integration Patterns (how to configure IMAP, SMTP, CalDAV, etc.), Generated Directory Structure (from ARCHITECTURE.md 'What Gets Generated')
For each schema field, document: name, type, required/optional, default, description, example value
acceptanceCriteria: docs/CONFIGURATION.md exists and documents the full blueprint YAML schema, skill config.yaml schema, SKILL.md format, and all 14 landmine rules
Every blueprint schema field includes: name, type, required/optional, default, description
Every skill config.yaml field includes: name, type, required/optional, default, description
The 14 landmine rules each have an explanation and the safe default ClawHQ enforces
A user can write a custom blueprint or skill using only this document as reference
items.groomingNotes: Documentation authoring task. Content sources confirmed: 7 blueprint YAMLs, 6 skill config.yaml files, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md (10-dimension table, 14 Landmine Rules), docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md (sections 10-12, 14), CLAUDE.md. No technical dependencies. Priority SHOULD is correct — QUICKSTART (FEAT-007) is higher urgency. Marking readyForSprint=true as requested by PO.

items.title: Create docs/PROBLEMS.md — why OpenClaw is hard and what ClawHQ fixes
items.intent: People search for problems, not solutions — this document must rank for the frustrations that drive users to ClawHQ.
items.description: The pain point content is the richest of any doc area but scattered across 4 files: docs/PRODUCT.md ('The Problem' section: freelancers/enthusiasts abandoned within a month, too much config), README.md (second paragraph: 13,500 tokens of config, 11+ files, 14 silent landmines, most deployments abandoned), docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md (section 12: the 14 Configuration Landmines with detailed explanations of each; section 8: Memory System 360KB bloat in 3 days; section 9: Identity Drift; section 15: Threat Model showing security-by-default gaps; section 30: Production Discoveries documenting real-world failures), CLAUDE.md (Core Intent: surveillance AI vs raw framework framing). This is primarily a consolidation and narrative job. Each problem should be stated as a user would google it, with evidence from OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md production discoveries, and a concrete explanation of how ClawHQ addresses it.
steps: Read docs/PRODUCT.md 'The Problem' section for the core problem statement
Read README.md second paragraph for the quantified pain (13,500 tokens, 11+ files, 14 landmines, abandoned deployments)
Read docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md section 12 (14 Configuration Landmines) for detailed landmine documentation
Read docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md section 8 (Memory System: 360KB bloat), section 9 (Identity Drift), section 15 (Threat Model), section 30 (Production Discoveries)
Read CLAUDE.md Core Intent section for the surveillance-vs-sovereignty framing

Compose docs/PROBLEMS.md with one section per problem, each structured as: search-friendly heading, pain description (2-3 sentences), evidence (from OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md production data or mark TODO), how ClawHQ addresses it (specific feature/blueprint/automation)

Cover at minimum: OpenClaw configuration is 13,500 tokens across 11+ files, 14 silent configuration landmines that break agents without warning, Memory bloats to 360KB in 3 days without management, Identity drift degrades agent quality over time, No security hardening by default (cap_drop, egress, rootfs), Most OpenClaw deployments abandoned within a month, People paying consultants to install what should be a consumer product
Write problem headings as search queries: e.g. 'OpenClaw configuration is too complex for non-developers' not 'Configuration Complexity'
acceptanceCriteria: docs/PROBLEMS.md exists with at least 7 distinct problem sections
Each problem is stated as a user would search for it (search-friendly heading)
Each problem section includes: pain description, evidence from OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md or real-world data, and how ClawHQ addresses it
Quantified claims (13,500 tokens, 14 landmines, 360KB bloat, 11+ files) are included with source attribution
Problems reference specific ClawHQ features (blueprints, auto-hardening, clawhq doctor, memory management) not vague claims
items.groomingNotes: Documentation authoring task. Content sources confirmed: docs/PRODUCT.md, README.md (quantified claims), docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md (sections 8, 9, 12, 15, 30), CLAUDE.md. Search-query headings are the right approach. No technical dependencies. Marking readyForSprint=true as requested by PO.

items.title: Create docs/ROADMAP.md — public-facing roadmap from internal planning docs
items.intent: Contributors and evaluators must see where the project is going and what's real vs aspirational — a project with no visible roadmap looks abandoned.
items.description: Roadmap content is rich but entirely internal-facing: docs/PRODUCT.md ('What We're Building' with 6 modules and checkboxes, 'Build Order' with Tracks A-F, 'How the Agent Grows' vision timeline for weeks/months, 'Risks & Open Questions'), backlog/GAP-ANALYSIS.md ('GAP ANALYSIS' Gaps 1-10 documenting technical gaps, 'REMEDIATION PRIORITY' Phases 1-3 with phased delivery plan, 'AS-IS: What's Built' subsystem status table with line counts). This content needs reframing from internal planning language to public-facing roadmap. Organize as: What's Built (current state from GAP-ANALYSIS AS-IS), Now (active work from todo backlog items), Next (committed direction from PRODUCT.md Build Order), Later (vision from PRODUCT.md 'How the Agent Grows'), Known Limitations (honest constraints from GAP-ANALYSIS gaps), Risks (from PRODUCT.md 'Risks & Open Questions'). Keep under 120 lines.
steps: Read docs/PRODUCT.md sections: 'What We're Building' (6 modules with status), 'Build Order' (Tracks A-F), 'How the Agent Grows' (week/month timeline), 'Risks & Open Questions'
Read backlog/GAP-ANALYSIS.md sections: 'AS-IS: What's Built' (subsystem status), 'GAP ANALYSIS' (Gaps 1-10), 'REMEDIATION PRIORITY' (Phases 1-3)
Review backlog/backlog.json for remaining todo items (CQ-047, FEAT-005) to populate the 'Now' section

Compose docs/ROADMAP.md with sections: What's Built (summarize AS-IS subsystem table), Now (active backlog items), Next (Track A-C from Build Order — platform, blueprints, security), Later (Tracks D-F — operate, evolve, cloud + the 'How the Agent Grows' vision), Known Limitations (from GAP-ANALYSIS gaps: e.g. single-machine only, no Windows support, requires Docker), Risks (from PRODUCT.md — honest about what might not work)
Reframe all internal language to public-facing: 'CQ-047' becomes 'Cloud deployment command', 'Track A' becomes descriptive feature names
Keep under 120 lines — signpost, not a product spec
acceptanceCriteria: docs/ROADMAP.md exists with sections: What's Built, Now, Next, Later, Known Limitations, Risks
Internal IDs and track names are translated to user-facing descriptions
Known Limitations is honest about real constraints
The document is under 120 lines
Each roadmap item is a clear statement with enough context to understand scope
items.groomingNotes: Documentation authoring task. Content sources confirmed: docs/PRODUCT.md (Tracks A-F, How the Agent Grows, Risks), backlog/GAP-ANALYSIS.md (AS-IS table, Gaps 1-10, Phases 1-3), backlog/backlog.json (todo items). 120-line cap is correct. Internal IDs must be translated to user-facing language. No technical dependencies. Marking readyForSprint=true as requested by PO.

items.title: Create docs/CONTRIBUTING.md — how to contribute blueprints, skills, and code
items.intent: A potential contributor must know exactly how to help — what's needed, what format to follow, and what the quality bar is. No CONTRIBUTING.md means no contributions.
items.description: No CONTRIBUTING.md exists. The content must be written from scratch but patterns can be derived from: configs/blueprints/*.yaml (7 blueprints establishing the blueprint contribution format), configs/skills/*/SKILL.md + config.yaml (6 skills establishing the skill contribution format), docs/ARCHITECTURE.md (Architectural Decisions AD-01 through AD-05 establishing constraints contributors must respect), CLAUDE.md (Terminology table for naming conventions, Key Design Constraints for invariants). Cover three contribution types: blueprints (write a new blueprint YAML following the schema, test against landmine validator), skills (write SKILL.md + config.yaml + prompts/, follow boundary enforcement rules), and code (TypeScript conventions, branch strategy, PR process). Include a 'What's Needed' section listing the highest-impact contributions — e.g. 'we need blueprints for: stock trading, home automation, content creation' to guide contributors toward valuable work.
steps: Read configs/blueprints/email-manager.yaml as the reference blueprint format for contributors
Read configs/skills/email-digest/SKILL.md, config.yaml, and prompts/ as the reference skill format
Read docs/ARCHITECTURE.md Architectural Decisions section (AD-01 through AD-05) for constraints
Read CLAUDE.md Terminology table and Key Design Constraints for naming conventions and invariants

Compose docs/CONTRIBUTING.md with sections: Overview (three ways to contribute: blueprints, skills, code), Blueprint Contribution Guide (YAML schema, required fields, testing with landmine validator, example walkthrough), Skill Contribution Guide (SKILL.md format, config.yaml schema, prompt template patterns, boundary enforcement requirements), Code Contribution Guide (TypeScript conventions, branch strategy, PR process, testing requirements), What's Needed (list of high-impact contributions: specific blueprint ideas, skill ideas, tooling gaps), Architectural Constraints (link to AD-01 through AD-05 in ARCHITECTURE.md), Security Requirements (boundary enforcement for skills, no network unless declared, egress rules)
acceptanceCriteria: docs/CONTRIBUTING.md exists and covers: blueprint contribution guide, skill contribution guide, code contribution guide, what's needed, architectural constraints
Blueprint guide includes the YAML schema with required fields and a worked example
Skill guide includes SKILL.md format, config.yaml schema, and boundary enforcement rules
A 'What's Needed' section lists specific high-impact contribution opportunities
Security requirements for contributed skills are clearly stated
items.groomingNotes: Documentation authoring task. Content sources confirmed: configs/blueprints/email-manager.yaml, configs/skills/email-digest/, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md (ADs 01-05), CLAUDE.md. The What's Needed section is highest-value addition. No technical dependencies. Marking readyForSprint=true as requested by PO.

items.title: Create docs/CHANGELOG.md — retroactive changelog from build history
items.intent: A project with no changelog looks like it was built in one shot — the changelog proves sustained development and helps evaluators gauge velocity and direction.
items.description: No CHANGELOG.md exists and no version tags are in the repo. However, build history can be reconstructed from: backlog/GAP-ANALYSIS.md 'AS-IS: What's Built' (subsystem status table with line counts showing what has shipped), docs/PRODUCT.md checkbox items showing completed features, backlog/archive/sprints.jsonl (sprint completion records with dates and item IDs), and backlog/backlog.json + bugs.json (done items with completedAt timestamps). Create a retroactive changelog organized by development phase rather than version numbers (since no versions exist yet). Phase 1: Foundation (config schema, landmine validator, gateway client, workspace generator). Phase 2: Blueprints (blueprint engine, 7 blueprints, skill system, 6 skills). Phase 3: Platform (install pipeline, security hardening, doctor/status, cron engine). Include dates where available from completedAt timestamps.
steps: Read backlog/GAP-ANALYSIS.md 'AS-IS: What's Built' section for the subsystem status table
Read backlog/backlog.json done items with completedAt timestamps to establish timeline
Read backlog/archive/sprints.jsonl for sprint completion records with dates
Read docs/PRODUCT.md 'What We're Building' checkbox items for completed feature list
Compose docs/CHANGELOG.md organized by development phase: Phase 1 Foundation (with dates from completedAt), Phase 2 Blueprints, Phase 3 Platform, using Added/Changed/Fixed format within each phase
Include line counts from GAP-ANALYSIS where available to show scope of each phase
acceptanceCriteria: docs/CHANGELOG.md exists with retroactive entries organized by development phase
Each phase lists completed features with dates where available
Entries use Added/Changed/Fixed format consistent with Keep a Changelog convention
The changelog accurately reflects what has been built — no aspirational entries
items.groomingNotes: Documentation authoring task. Content sources confirmed: backlog/GAP-ANALYSIS.md (AS-IS subsystem table with line counts), backlog/archive/done.json and backlog/archive/backlog_done.json (completed items with dates), docs/PRODUCT.md (completed checkbox items). Phase-based organisation is correct since no semver versions exist. Priority COULD is correct. No technical dependencies. Marking readyForSprint=true as requested by PO.

items.title: Slim README.md to storefront format — problem, solution, quickstart, links
items.intent: The README must sell the project in 60 seconds — the current README is a product document pretending to be a landing page.
items.description: The current README.md is a comprehensive product document containing architecture diagrams, deployment tables, data sovereignty matrices, CLI command listings, and blueprint dimension tables. This content belongs in docs/ files (most of which already exist in docs/ARCHITECTURE.md, docs/PRODUCT.md, docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md, or will be created by FEAT-006 through FEAT-012). After the docs/ files exist, the README must be cut to storefront format: one-sentence pitch ('WordPress for AI agents'), the problem (13,500 tokens, 14 landmines, abandoned deployments — 3 sentences max from the existing second paragraph), the solution (what ClawHQ does — 1 paragraph), quick start (install + init — 3 commands max, link to docs/QUICKSTART.md), the blueprint concept (1 paragraph + simple example, link to docs/CONFIGURATION.md), status (honest assessment), links section (all docs/ files), author section. Remove: How It Works detail, The Platform CLI listings, Blueprints dimension table, Cloud section, Deployment Options table, Data Sovereignty table, Why This Exists (fold into problem/solution). All removed content must have a home in a docs/ file.
steps: Verify that docs/ARCHITECTURE.md (with FEAT-006 skill section), docs/QUICKSTART.md, docs/CONFIGURATION.md, docs/PROBLEMS.md, docs/ROADMAP.md, docs/CONTRIBUTING.md, and docs/CHANGELOG.md all exist
Keep in README.md: opening pitch ('WordPress for AI agents'), the problem paragraph (13,500 tokens, 14 landmines, abandoned deployments), the solution paragraph ('ClawHQ turns generic...')
Keep in README.md: the three-layer ASCII diagram from How It Works (Cloud/Blueprints/Platform) but remove all detail below it
Add abbreviated Quick Start: install one-liner, clawhq init --guided, clawhq up — then link to docs/QUICKSTART.md
Add one blueprint example (email-manager in 3 lines) then link to docs/CONFIGURATION.md
Add Status section with honest assessment
Add Links section listing every docs/ file with one-line description
Add Author section: Simon Plant, building AI infrastructure tools, open to roles
Remove: The Platform (CLI listings), Blueprints (dimension table, evolution commands), Cloud (managed hosting details), Deployment Options table, Data Sovereignty table, Why This Exists section
Verify all removed content exists in a docs/ file — nothing lost
acceptanceCriteria: README.md is under 150 lines (current is 300+)
README.md contains only: pitch, problem, solution, three-layer diagram, quick start (abbreviated), one blueprint example, status, links, author
Every section removed from README.md has a corresponding docs/ file containing the content
Links section includes: Architecture, Quickstart, Configuration, Problems, Roadmap, Contributing, Changelog
No content is lost — everything is in README.md or linked from it
items.groomingNotes: Dependency chain is correct and must be enforced: FEAT-006 through FEAT-012 must all ship before README slim-down. The README currently serves as the only comprehensive doc — cutting it before docs/ replacements exist would break the user journey. Priority MUST is correct for this final state: a 300-line README that acts as a product doc is a first-impression failure. The 150-line target is achievable given the content has a home in docs/. Author section content confirmed from CLAUDE.md. NOT READY until all 7 dependencies are done — do not sprint this item early.

items.title: Add .doc-standard.yaml schema file for doc structure enforcement
items.intent: The repo must declare its doc standard version and structure so automated checks can validate compliance — undeclared standards are unenforceable.
items.description: Part of a cross-repo documentation standard shared with aishore, GigOS, and other portfolio repos. The .doc-standard.yaml declares: schema version, project metadata (name: ClawHQ, tagline: WordPress for AI agents, category: infrastructure), required files list matching the docs/ structure, cross-repo links to sibling projects (aishore, Easy-Markdown, GigOS), and author info for consistency enforcement across the portfolio. This file enables a future GitHub Action to validate doc structure compliance on PR. Note: ClawHQ has additional docs not in the standard (docs/PRODUCT.md, docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md) — these should be listed in an optional_files section so the validator doesn't flag them as non-standard.
steps: Create .doc-standard.yaml at repo root
Set schema_version to '1.0'
Set project name to 'ClawHQ', tagline to 'WordPress for AI agents', category to 'infrastructure'
List required_files: README.md, docs/ARCHITECTURE.md, docs/QUICKSTART.md, docs/CONFIGURATION.md, docs/PROBLEMS.md, docs/ROADMAP.md, docs/CONTRIBUTING.md, docs/CHANGELOG.md
Add optional_files: docs/PRODUCT.md, docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md (repo-specific docs not in the standard)
Add portfolio section with sibling repos: aishore (complementary), Easy-Markdown (related), GigOS (related)
Add author section: name Simon Plant, location Santa Barbara CA, status 'Building AI infrastructure tools. Open to roles.'
Verify the file is valid YAML
acceptanceCriteria: .doc-standard.yaml exists at repo root and is valid YAML
File declares schema_version, project metadata, required_files, optional_files, portfolio siblings, and author info
required_files matches the docs/ structure created by FEAT-006 through FEAT-012
optional_files lists docs/PRODUCT.md and docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md so they are not flagged as non-standard
Portfolio section lists aishore, Easy-Markdown, and GigOS with correct GitHub URLs
